Since this is a rough calculation and does not pretend to a high level of accuracy, given the underlying circumstances, it's fair to conclude, in my view, that if the stock price falls into the low $30 price range, that would be the moment to take a more serious look at what has happened meantime and whether there are signs of better visibility.

Dividend Sustainability

As is sometimes the case, companies pay dividends even when ongoing profitability isn't high enough to support them. This is often achieved using previously accumulated liquidity reserves or even by taking on more debt, if the underlying financials allow it. In either case, the company expects better times ahead and aims to be shareholder-friendly.

As I wrote at the beginning of this analysis, I have specific criteria I use to assess whether a dividend can be considered sustainable and safe. I will now go through these points for the benefit of long-term dividend investors who don't want to time the market and instead choose to hold, believing the cycle will sooner or later turn around.

Cash Flow

The first half of 2025 doesn't look good, as the cash outflow of $1.25 billion is definitely a weak result:

The CFO downplayed the weak showing in the earnings call:

Cash used in operating activities of $228 million in the first six months of 2025 primarily reflected earnings adjusted for non-cash items, $384 million of tax payments which includes $235 million U.S. Federal corporate income tax payments deferred from 2024 into 2025 under Hurricane Beryl disaster relief, and a $433 million change in Accounts payable which was driven by the timing of payments.

The following table shows how the company utilized cash in its financing activities. Specifically, $201 million was used for share buybacks, and $878 million went to dividends. Additionally, $499 million in new debt was taken on.

To summarize here, the combined outflows for the 1H 2025: negative $1.25 billion in free cash flow, plus $1.079 billion paid out to shareholders (these two combined $2.329 billion), do not seem sustainable to me. The CFO provided his view on this situation when questioned by an analyst at the earnings call:

And I would also further point that our cash generation and cash conversion is usually much stronger in the second half of the year. So we are expecting -- if you recall, last year, the second half had a cash generation or cash conversion of 167% and just in Q4 alone, more than 280%.

So we are on target for strong cash conversion as well on the second half of the year. We expect positive cash flow from operations also in the second half of the year, and we are still on target to have our 80% cash conversion full year.

Should the situation play out as the CFO predicts, it would definitely soften the concerning results seen in the first half of the year. In addition, management presented the following actions being taken to improve the cash flow outlook:

The next slide presents the balance sheet's cash dynamics for Q2.

The $500 million in new debt ($499 million, to be exact, as per the SEC filing) was crucial, as the ending cash balance would otherwise have been approximately $1.2 billion. To put this in context, LYB needs approximately $445 million for the next dividend payment and an additional $490 million to cover maturing debt by year-end. If we assume cash flow doesn't improve in the next two quarters, these outflows would have nearly drained their cash reserves without the $500 million in new debt they took on during Q2.

Net debt/EBITDA

The very latest multiple and its underlying calculation have been taken directly from LYB’s SEC filing:

The ratio was quite close to the covenant previously agreed upon with LYB’s bankers. Had the financials deteriorated and the ratio climbed above, the company would have been restricted by lenders from paying a dividend. Consequently, as of September 10, 2025, the company signed an amendment to that agreement with its lenders to raise the covenant ratio as follows:

This means that LYB can proceed with dividends without restrictions from its financiers as long as the Net Debt/EBITDA ratio remains below these new multiples.

LYB's Debt Profile

In my view, it's important to highlight how the debt markets view the company's prospects: LYB's 10-year notes, recently issued at a yield of 6.15%, compare unfavorably to the 5.5% secured for the same duration last year. This suggests that debt markets are also pricing in higher risk. From LYB’s SEC filing:

In May 2025, we issued $500 million of 6.150% guaranteed notes due 2035. Net proceeds from the sale of the notes are used for general corporate purposes, including the repayment of our 2025 Notes later in the year. In February 2024, we issued $750 million of 5.5% guaranteed notes due 2034.

To add a bit more color to the overall debt profile: While the debt maturity profile is well-structured, the upcoming refinancing will be significantly more expensive. This is particularly true since the very next maturities are currently priced at really low yields (1.25% and 0.875%).

All in all, the debt profile is great, with nothing alarming in the near term. The key takeaway is that the next two refinancings will come with significantly higher price tags. My rough, rounded math shows that an additional 5% yield on $1 billion in debt will cost approximately $50 million more annually. While this is not overly significant for a company of LYB's scale, it still represents an additional ongoing cost.

Dividend History

It dates back to 2011, and it's important to note that since then, LYB has never cut the payout. Furthermore, the company has established a practice of regular annual dividend increases, which began in 2021. This commitment helps explain why management raised the dividend back in May of this year—marking the fifth consecutive annual increase—despite a clearly worsening business outlook at the time.

Management's Last Comments About Dividends

During the Q2 earnings call, CEO Peter Vanacker provided the following answer when asked by an analyst about his view on the dividend:

As part of our overall capital allocation strategy, we plan to provide returns to shareholders in the form of dividends and share repurchases. Barring any significant or unforeseen business challenges, mergers or acquisitions, over the long-term, we are targeting shareholder returns of 70% of free cash flow, defined as net cash provided by operating activities less capital expenditures. We intend to continue to declare and pay quarterly dividends, including a third quarter 2025 dividend of $1.37 per share. We have a goal of increasing the dividend over time, after giving consideration to our cash balances and expected results from operations. Our focus on funding our dividends while remaining committed to an investment grade balance sheet continues to be the foundation of our capital allocation strategy.

So in that context, we continue to recognize the importance of the dividend as a significant component of our returns to shareholders, and our actions continue to focus on securing our dividend while maintaining the benefits of our investment-grade rating. We're also not planning any further share buybacks over 2025 and 2026. We did some, as you know, in Q1. We did some in Q2. And our cash improvement plan is on track to improve our cash flow over the years 2025 and 2026, as set by at least, I mean, $1.1 billion.In addition to that, I mean, we believe the cycle will eventually rebound.

Risks

In my view, the company faces a significant feedstock risk: the AI boom is increasing energy demand, which has driven up natural gas prices. This is crucial, as natural gas is the main pillar of LYB's profitability and competitiveness.

I also see a political risk: if Chinese authorities view the sector as strategically important and choose to maintain or expand their market share, they could drive prices down over the long term, regardless of economic rationale.

Outside of that, I don't see LYB facing any other particular concentrated risk, as it is well-diversified across both markets and products. The primary challenge lies in the execution through the ongoing, serious downcycle and the length and depth of that cycle. Management must make prudent decisions to successfully navigate the company through these 'wild seas' to calmer waters.

The well-spoken macro and tariff risks are not specific to LYB but apply equally to any company in the sector.

The dividend is another key area that requires careful handling. While maintaining the dividend is certainly very important from a shareholder-friendly approach and long-standing traditional standpoint, however, from the perspective of the company's long-term financial sustainability, it might be better to step back on that front until the business cycle shows clear signs of improvement.

Takeaway

My view is that while LyodelBasell is a solid company, the cycle they are currently exposed not an easy one to get through. Management will likely try to keep the dividend, and as it said, “fight until the very end.” The latest SEC filing about covenants rise is great support for that thesis. Seems to me that if nothing extraordinary occurs and CFO projections about better cash flow hold water, investors likely receive at least the next quarter or two dividends at the current extraordinary high level.

However, the upcoming next quarter's cash flow statement and management’s comments have to be carefully listened to and will definitely give some additional clarity on what can be expected. If real improvements in the downcycle are not evident, management's ability to outmaneuver the weakening liquidity profile and leverage metrics without cutting the dividend will be limited in my view. Cost and CapEx cuts alone will not provide enough financial capacity to maintain the dividend at the current level.

data.title: Central Garden And Pet Traded Growth For Major Margin Gains
data.description:
Investors aren’t confident about Central Garden and Pet (NASDAQ:CENT), which makes pet food and lawn supplies, right now. The company’s stock price has fallen 17% in the past month. Its sales are falling, so it may look like it’s a declining business. But investors might not understand what the company is really doing and why it’s willing to give up potential sales.

For the third quarter of 2025, Central Garden and Pet posted $961 million in sales, a 3.6% decline. While this looks bad, it also reported that adjusted EPS rose 18% to $1.52. It also missed analysts’ estimates on revenue but beat their estimates on the bottom line. And performance like this is likely to continue for Central Garden and Pet. The company is currently implementing its Cost and Simplicity program, a long-term efficiency plan that’s designed to lower its costs.

Central Garden and Pet is willing to exit lower-margin markets and give up unprofitable business lines to raise its margins and get its costs down. As a result, it’s likely to post weaker revenue in the short term. But in the longer term, Central Garden and Pet has been able to expand by acquiring other pet food and garden products manufacturers, and a stronger balance sheet will allow the company to grow sales by making acquisitions in the future. There are several factors to consider here, so I’ll start by explaining why the company’s sales dropped and its profits rose this quarter.

Central Garden and Pet Is Exiting Less Profitable Markets

During the 3Q 2025 earnings call, Central Garden and Pet listed several markets that it’s exiting. It has decided to stop producing pet products in Britain and is selling its operations there. It’s also stopped producing lower-margin durable pet products and is focusing on consumables instead. And it stopped selling two garden product lines that were produced by a third-party manufacturer. The company also made cutbacks in its live plants business. These decisions caused Central Garden and Pet to incur additional shutdown costs for these operations as well.

Central Garden and Pet’s TTM operating margin is 8.1% according to Seeking Alpha, but its margin rose significantly this quarter. The company reported that during the third quarter of 2025, its operating margin was 14.1%, up 250 basis points from 11.6% in the third quarter of 2024. It appears that shutting down lower-margin business lines had a large impact on this quarter’s results.

The company is also shutting down two of its old distribution facilities and building a new distribution center in Salt Lake City. On the call, CEO Nicholas Lahanas said that the Salt Lake City distribution center would be operational in a month, which means it began operating in September during the company’s fourth fiscal quarter of 2025. The distribution center is designed to ship orders to DTC customers as well as the other stores that sell Central Garden and Pet’s products, such as pet stores and home improvement stores.

Organic Growth Is Unlikely in the Short Term for Central Pet and Garden

With that said, the company’s decision to shut down several operations and product lines is likely to result in negative revenue growth for the next few quarters. Central Garden and Pet doesn’t appear to have any organic revenue growth right now, and it’s unlikely to report any organic growth in the short term because of the overall market for pet products as well. I think some analysts might have bearish views on Central Garden and Pet because they think demand for pet products is falling, so I’ll explain why I consider the market stable right now.

In my recent article about the online store Chewy (CHWY), which is one of Central Garden and Pet’s customers, I went into more detail about the state of the pet products market. The Chewy CEO says that pet household formation is stable right now, so pet food sales should also be relatively stable. Pets still need to eat. And while private-label pet food is a potential threat to Central Garden and Pet’s market, the company has strong brands and Chewy’s private-label brands target high-income customers.

Central Garden and Pet also raised its guidance and called for non-GAAP EPS of $2.60 per share in the third-quarter earnings report. The company didn’t provide any 2025 guidance for revenue, which is unusual because most companies do that. But again, it reported -3.6% revenue growth in the third quarter and it posted -7.4% revenue growth in the second quarter. It’s likely that Central Garden and Pet expects negative revenue growth in the fourth quarter as well and doesn’t want to include that in its guidance. But I think it’s important to consider the bottom line for this company in particular because of the possibility of inorganic revenue growth in 2026.

Acquisitions Are a Possibility in 2026

Lahanas did discuss Central Garden and Pet’s acquisition strategy on the third-quarter 2025 earnings call. It sounds like management doesn’t see many great acquisition opportunities right now, but also feels like the business environment is improving. So the company plans to look for more acquisition opportunities next year and it’s looking for companies that manufacture consumables. For Central Garden and Pet, this category includes products such as grass seeds and bird seed as well as dog and cat food.

Central Garden and Pet’s last major acquisition was the purchase of the premium pet treat manufacturer TDBBS from the private equity firm Bregal Partners in 2023. TDBBS makes chewable treats such as bully sticks and jerky for dogs. On the call, management brought up the TDBBS acquisition and sounded confident about it. TDBBS has now been successfully integrated into Central Garden and Pet’s operations and Central Garden and Pet expects it to improve its future results as well.

It’s possible that Central Garden and Pet will attempt to buy another small pet food manufacturer that makes premium products such as health food for pets in 2026. This would give the company more access to rapidly growing market segments that have higher margins, and because premium pet food is consumable it would also gain repeat customers. But again, Central Garden faces competition from private-label products manufactured by its customers, such as Chewy, in this market as well.

Risks

Tariffs are a major risk factor for Central Garden and Pet. Like other manufacturers, it buys many of its products from China. On the call, the company explained that high inventory levels protected it from tariffs to some extent in 2025, but it now expects a $10 million impact from tariffs in the fourth quarter. It sounds like management plans to raise prices on its products and find suppliers outside of China to reduce the impact of tariffs in 2026.

The company-specific risk for Central Garden and Pet is that pet owners might decide to conserve their budgets by buying private-label food from pet stores. Gardeners might also decide to buy private-label products instead of brand-name ones. Central Garden and Pet does manufacture private-label products itself, so it won’t necessarily lose customers if this happens, but private-label products will have lower margins than its brand-name products.

Comparative Valuation

As a company with falling revenue, Central Garden and Pet is trading at low valuations. Its forward EV/sales ratio is 0.78, 46% lower than the median for household products makers. Its forward EV/EBITDA is 6.80, 35% below the household products median. It’s also trading at a price-to-book value ratio of 1.17.

A discount is justified because of Central Garden and Pet’s poor top-line results, but investors might not be fully considering the fact that this company also plans to continue making acquisitions and has the cash to do it.

Calculating Central Garden and Pet’s Value

Central Garden and Pet has raised its EPS guidance, and after calling for $2.20 in non-GAAP EPS in the second quarter, in the third quarter it said it now expects $2.60 in non-GAAP EPS. I’ll estimate that its revenue for 2025 will be $3.1 billion, which is a 3% decrease year-over-year. It has 64.9 million diluted shares outstanding so its net income would be $169 million for the full year. It also bought back 2 million shares of stock in 2025 because it didn’t find a suitable acquisition target.

Because Central Garden and Pet discontinued several businesses and is unlikely to grow organically next year, I’ll project a 3% fall in revenue to $3.0 billion for full-year 2026. The company's operating margin rose from 11.2% in the second quarter to 14.1% in the third quarter of 2025. It also said that its non-GAAP operating margin for the quarter was 14.5%. If I project that its full-year operating margin could be 12.5% next year, and subtract 50 basis points for tariff impact, at a 12% margin the company would post $360 million in operating profit. If I subtract $30 million in net interest expenses because the company appears to be paying down debt and assume a 25% tax rate, that’s $248 million in net income.

At a 2026 forward P/E of 12, Central Garden and Pet would have a market cap of $2.98 billion. If I assume that Central Garden and Pet doesn’t find a company to acquire, it could buy back 2 million shares in 2026, which would reduce its share count to 62.9 million. Dividing a market cap of $2.98 billion by 62.9 million shares results in a target price of $47 per share. Central Garden and Pet’s closing price was $30 on October 15, so my price target is 57% higher than the current price.

Wall Street analysts currently have a target price of $42 for Central Garden and Pet, which is 43% higher than the current price. SA analysts currently rate this stock a hold, Wall Street analysts rate it a buy, and the SA quant score says it’s a hold.

Conclusion

This is going to be a contrarian call, but I’d say that Central Garden and Pet is a strong buy. It appears that investors are only looking at its top-line results and not its bottom-line performance, and I consider the recent selloff unjustified because of this factor. By shutting down lower-margin product lines and exiting unprofitable markets, Central Garden and Pet has made major improvements to its margins. And even though its organic revenue is expected to fall, this company has the cash to acquire another pet food manufacturer and can grow sales that way as well.
